Excellent! I now have the full article text. This is the critical piece of evidence. Let me now compile the complete research report.

---

# NASA X-59 Quesst Flight Test Schedule — June 2026 Mach 1 Research Report

## Executive Summary

As of June 1, 2026, NASA's X-59 quiet supersonic research aircraft is **imminently preparing to break the sound barrier for the first time** in early June 2026. A NASA article published **May 28, 2026** confirms the X-59 is entering a new block of test flights that will include its first supersonic flight at approximately **43,000 feet and over 630 mph**, followed by a "mission conditions" flight at **Mach 1.4 (925 mph) at 55,000 feet**.

## Key Milestones Achieved

### Flight History Timeline
- **January 12, 2024**: X-59 publicly unveiled at Lockheed Martin Skunk Works, Palmdale, CA
- **November 2024**: First engine run
- **July 17, 2025**: First self-powered taxi test
- **July 25, 2025**: Ground instrument calibration test using F-15B
- **October 28, 2025**: **First flight** — from Air Force Plant 42 to NASA Armstrong Flight Research Center (Edwards AFB). Subsonic: ~230 mph at 12,000 feet altitude, ~1 hour duration.
- **Post-October 2025**: Scheduled maintenance period
- **March 2026**: Returned to flight operations
- **April 3, 2026**: **First wheels-up flight** — reached 20,000 feet and ~460 mph
- **March–May 2026**: Completed **14 additional flights** (15 total since returning in March), expanding the envelope in speed and altitude
- **May 12, 2026**: Flight over Rogers Dry Lake near NASA Armstrong (photo from this flight used in NASA article)
- **Late May 2026**: Team reviewed flight test progress and approved next block of flights

### Current Status (as of May 28, 2026)
The X-59 has completed Phase 1 subsonic/transonic envelope expansion and is about to begin a new flight test block that will include:

1. **First supersonic flight (Mach 1+)**: Expected in **early June 2026** at ~43,000 feet altitude, exceeding 630 mph
2. **First "mission conditions" flight**: Mach 1.4 (925 mph) at ~55,000 feet — the design cruise condition
3. **Maximum speed test**: Mach 1.6 (1,218 mph)
4. **Maximum altitude**: 60,000 feet

## Flight Test Program Structure

### Phase 1 (Current): Performance & Airworthiness
- Proving the aircraft's performance and airworthiness
- Envelope expansion from subsonic through supersonic speeds
- Includes early deployment of shock-sensing probe on NASA F-15 chase aircraft
- **Note**: These flights are NOT yet demonstrating quiet supersonic capability — a traditional supersonic chase plane will accompany X-59, producing conventional sonic booms that will mask the X-59's thump

### Phase 2 (Expected later in 2026): Acoustic Validation
- Begins measuring the X-59's supersonic flight signature
- Verify the aircraft produces a quiet supersonic "thump" (~75 EPNdB) rather than a conventional sonic boom (~105+ EPNdB)
- Will use ground-based acoustic sensors and the F-15-mounted shock-sensing probe

### Phase 3 (Future — likely 2027–2028): Community Overflights
- Supersonic flights over select U.S. communities
- Gather public response data on the quiet sonic thump
- Data submitted to FAA and ICAO to inform potential rule changes on overland supersonic commercial flight
- Results originally expected to be delivered to ICAO/FAA by ~2027, with a regulatory decision possible by ~2028

## Aircraft Specifications
| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Length | 99.7 ft (30.4 m) |
| Wingspan | 29.5 ft (9.0 m) |
| Max takeoff weight | 32,300 lb (14,700 kg) |
| Engine | GE F414-GE-100 (22,000 lbf thrust with afterburner) |
| Design cruise speed | Mach 1.42 (940 mph / 1,510 km/h) |
| Maximum speed | Mach 1.6 (1,218 mph) |
| Cruise altitude | 55,000 ft (16,800 m) |
| Max altitude | 60,000 ft |
| Target ground noise | ~75 EPNdB (vs. ~105–110 EPNdB for conventional sonic boom) |
| Vision system | eXternal Vision System (XVS) — camera-to-monitor, no forward windshield |

## Key Quotes from NASA Officials

**Cathy Bahm, Project Manager, NASA Low Boom Flight Demonstrator:**
- *"What comes next is the first time this one-of-a-kind aircraft will fly supersonic. We are starting toward the mission conditions test point that X-59 was designed for."*
- *"Flying at supersonic speeds is a major milestone for the X-59 team. Every step of envelope expansion brings us closer to demonstrating the quiet supersonic capability that is at the heart of the Quesst mission."*
- *"As we look ahead to the upcoming flights, we're poised to open the envelope even further – moving boldly toward the mission test point this aircraft was built to achieve."*

## Program History & Delays
- **Original first flight target**: 2021–2022
- **Contract awarded**: April 2018 ($247.5 million to Lockheed Martin)
- **Actual first flight**: October 28, 2025 (approximately 3–4 years behind original schedule)
- **Actual first supersonic flight**: Expected early June 2026

## Assessment for Forecasting

The evidence strongly indicates that the X-59 will fly at Mach 1+ for the first time in **early June 2026**. As of June 1, 2026:

- NASA published a major article on May 28, 2026 confirming the upcoming supersonic flight block
- The team has completed 15+ flights since March 2026, steadily expanding the envelope
- The project manager confirmed the aircraft is entering the supersonic flight block
- The Mach 1+ flight is expected at ~43,000 feet (first supersonic), with Mach 1.4 at 55,000 feet to follow
- Maximum speed of Mach 1.6 and altitude of 60,000 feet are also planned for this block
- Phase 2 acoustic validation is expected to begin "later this year" (2026)

sources.title: NASA's X-59 Prepares for First Supersonic Flight
sources.snippet: The team expects the X-59 to fly supersonic – over 630 mph – for the first time at approximately 43,000 feet altitude during a series of test flights in early June, a major milestone for the aircraft. After that, it will conduct a 'mission conditions' flight, where it will hit Mach 1.4 (925 mph) at approximately 55,000 feet.

sources.title: Lockheed Martin X-59 Quesst - Wikipedia
sources.snippet: The X-59 took its first flight in the morning of October 28, 2025, from Air Force Plant 42, and landed around an hour later at NASA's Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards AFB. On April 3, 2026 the X-59 completed its first wheels up flight, reaching 20,000 feet and approximately 460 mph.

sources.snippet: NASA's Quesst mission, which features the one-of-a-kind X-59 aircraft, will demonstrate technology to fly supersonic, or faster than the speed of sound, without generating loud sonic booms.

The X-59 made its first flight on October 28, 2025, from Air Force Plant 42, reaching 230 mph at 12,000 feet subsonic
The X-59 completed its first wheels-up flight on April 3, 2026, reaching 20,000 feet and ~460 mph
Since returning to flight in March 2026 after scheduled maintenance, the X-59 has completed 14 additional flights (15 total in this block)
First supersonic flight expected at ~43,000 feet altitude, exceeding 630 mph, in early June 2026
First 'mission conditions' flight planned at Mach 1.4 (925 mph) at 55,000 feet — the aircraft's design cruise condition
Maximum planned speed is Mach 1.6 (1,218 mph) and maximum altitude 60,000 feet, both to be tested in this flight block
Phase 2 (acoustic validation to verify quiet sonic thump) expected to begin later in 2026
Phase 3 (community overflights) planned for after Phase 2, likely 2027-2028; data to be delivered to FAA/ICAO to potentially revise rules on supersonic overland flight
Target ground noise level is ~75 EPNdB vs ~105-110 EPNdB for conventional sonic boom
The X-59 was built by Lockheed Martin Skunk Works under a $247.5 million contract awarded in April 2018
Early supersonic flights will be accompanied by a traditional supersonic chase plane, so the X-59's quiet thump will be masked by conventional sonic booms from the chase aircraft
An F-15 research aircraft will carry a shock-sensing probe to take initial measurements of the X-59's shock wave signature during supersonic flights this summer
